questions.question: Which instrument component determines how many fire-listen cycles occur each second?
options: The receiver's filter stage
The clock (timer/synchronizer), which sets the pulse repetition frequency
The gain control
The delay control
questions.explanation: The clock triggers each cycle and therefore sets the PRF; gain, filtering, and delay shape or position the signal but do not control firing rate.

questions.question: What is the difference between an RF display and a full-wave rectified display?
options: RF shows the raw waveform swinging above and below zero; full-wave rectification folds the negative half up into a single positive envelope
RF is for angle beam only; rectified is for straight beam
RF displays frequency instead of time
Full-wave rectification doubles the echo amplitude
questions.explanation: The receiver's rectifier converts the bipolar RF signal into the positive envelope most field instruments display; the underlying timing information is the same.

questions.question: What does a gate on a pulse-echo instrument do?
options: It increases pulser voltage during scanning
It monitors a selected span of the time base and triggers an alarm or outputs a measurement when an echo crosses its threshold
It blanks all signals below a set amplitude across the whole screen
It locks the gain so it cannot be changed
questions.explanation: A gate watches a chosen region of the sound path and reacts to signals inside it - the basis of flaw alarms and automatic thickness readouts. Blanking small signals everywhere is the reject control, not a gate.

questions.question: An indication needs 20 dB of added gain to reach the same screen height as your reference echo. Its amplitude relative to the reference is:
options: One half
One quarter
One tenth
One twentieth
questions.explanation: 20 dB corresponds to a factor of 10 in amplitude (20 log 10 = 20 dB), so the indication is returning one tenth of the reference amplitude.

questions.question: Periodic screen-height linearity verification confirms that:
options: The probe's refracted angle has not changed
A 6 dB gain change truly halves or doubles displayed echo height across the screen, so amplitude comparisons can be trusted
The couplant meets viscosity requirements
The PRF matches the part length
questions.explanation: Amplitude-based evaluation assumes display height scales truly with signal amplitude; codes such as ASME Section V Article 4 require periodic vertical and amplitude-control linearity checks to prove it.

questions.question: Halfway through a weld examination your probe cable fails and you swap in a spare cable. What does the procedure require?
options: Continue - cables do not affect calibration
Re-verify standardization before continuing, because a system component changed
Add 6 dB of gain to cover any difference
Note the change in the report and re-standardize at the end of the shift only
questions.explanation: Any change to the system - probe, cable, couplant type, significant temperature shift - invalidates the standardization, which must be re-verified before more data is collected.

questions.question: In an area-amplitude reference block set, a number 5 flat-bottom hole has a diameter of:
options: 5 mm
5/64 of an inch
0.5 inch
5/32 of an inch
questions.explanation: FBH numbers give the hole diameter in 64ths of an inch, so a #5 hole is 5/64 in. (about 2 mm); the set runs #1 through #8 at a fixed metal travel.

questions.question: A digital thickness gauge times a 6.76 µs round trip in carbon steel (about 5.92 mm/µs). The displayed thickness should be about:
options: 6.8 mm
questions.explanation: T = vt/2 = 5.92 x 6.76 / 2 = 20.0 mm. Forgetting the divide-by-two gives the 40 mm distractor.

questions.question: Why are dual-element probes the standard choice for corrosion-survey thickness gauging?
options: They produce shear waves, which measure thickness more accurately
Their crossed transmit and receive elements give a pseudo-focus just below the surface and almost no dead zone, ideal for rough, pitted back walls
They eliminate the need for couplant
They are the only probes that work in mode 3
questions.explanation: The slightly angled separate elements focus sensitivity where pitting lives and keep the receiver from being blinded by the transmit pulse; the trade-off is a small V-path nonlinearity on very thin walls.

questions.question: On a smooth, uncorroded section of plate, one isolated point suddenly reads about half the nominal wall. The most professional interpretation is:
options: Record it as wall loss - the gauge does not lie
It may be a mid-wall lamination returning an early echo rather than thinning; check the waveform, scan around the point, and report it for evaluation
It is doubling, so the true wall is twice the reading
The battery is low; replace it and keep the original reading
questions.explanation: A lamination reflects sound from mid-thickness and mimics sudden thinning. Doubling would read thicker, not thinner. Verifying with the waveform and surrounding readings separates metal loss from an internal discontinuity.
